title: Pinch-pot candle holder trio
subtitle: Three small air-dry clay cups sized for tea lights
excerpt: Three matching pinch-pot cups in air-dry clay, each sized to hold one standard tea light. The forms are organic and slightly irregular, which is the point. Walls are kept at least 8 mm thick throughout for safe heat handling. No kiln required.
sourceNotes: Pinch-pot construction is the oldest documented ceramic-forming method. Technique derived from first principles. Tea-light sizing: standard tea light is 38 mm diameter and 15 mm high.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pot formed by pressing the thumb into the centre of a clay ball and walking the fingers and thumb around the wall to thin and raise it.
Fully air-dried but not fired. For air-dry clay, the finished structural state.

content.text: A
pinch-pot

candle holder is one of the simplest useful ceramic forms. Each cup holds a single tea light, which sits in the base of the pot and radiates warmth through the clay walls. Use tea lights only: the flame inside a standard tea light stays well below the 60 mm rim height. Keep walls at least 8 mm thick throughout so the heat does not cause the air-dry clay to soften or crack. Air-dry clay does not handle open flame or candles taller than a tea light.

attrs.content: Use tea lights only. Walls must be at least 8 mm thick throughout. Never use taper candles, pillar candles, or anything with an exposed flame taller than the rim.

content.text: Forming one cup
1. Start the ball

Take 100 g of clay and roll it into a smooth ball between the palms. Press your thumb straight down into the centre of the ball, stopping 8 mm from the base. Rotate the ball a quarter-turn and press again, working around the ball until you have opened a central cavity 40 mm across and 40 mm deep.
2. Thin and raise the walls

Place the pot in the palm of one hand. Use the thumb inside and two fingers outside, working in a pinching motion, and walk your way around the wall rotating the pot as you go. Each pinch thins the wall slightly and raises it. Work in even rows from the base up to the rim, not concentrating too long on one spot. Target wall thickness: 8 mm. Check by pressing thumb and finger against either side; you should feel the resistance of the clay without breaking through.
3. Size the opening

Drop a tea light into the opening to check the fit. The tea light should sit in the base with 5-10 mm of clearance around it. If the opening is too tight, ease it open with your thumb while rotating. If the opening is too wide, gently press the rim inward.
4. Level the base
Turn the cup upside down and press the base lightly flat on the board to flatten the bottom so it sits without rocking. A slight press is enough; do not flatten so hard the base cracks.
5. Smooth the rim
Run the damp sponge once around the rim to soften any rough edges. Compress the rim slightly with the thumb and forefinger pinched together to close any small cracks from the pinching process.
Making the trio

Repeat the process for all three cups. Each form will be slightly different, which is the nature of pinch-pot work. You can vary the heights: 50 mm, 55 mm, and 60 mm, or vary the opening from snug to wide.
Drying and finishing
Place the cups upside down to dry for the first hour so gravity does not cause the opening to flare. Then turn right-way up and dry to
bone-dry

over two days. Sand any rough patches with 220-grit sandpaper. Paint with acrylics or leave plain. Seal the outside with two coats of PVA varnish; leave the inside unsealed so the clay breathes near the tea-light heat.
Variations
Use a pointed tool to pierce a pattern of small holes through the walls before drying. The tea light glows through the holes.
Press a small sprig of dried herbs into the outside wall while the clay is still soft for a botanical impression.
Make five instead of three and arrange in a cluster at different heights.

items.cause: Clay was allowed to dry out too much before finishing, or pinching was too aggressive in one spot
items.fix: Smooth cracks with a damp sponge and a little slurry. Work more quickly, rotating continuously to avoid over-pinching any one area

items.symptom: Base too thin and tea light shows through
items.cause: Thumb was pushed too deep into the ball at the start
items.fix: Stop the thumb 8 mm from the base. Feel the base thickness with the pad of the finger during pinching and stop thinning when it feels solid

items.cause: Pinching motion angled outward instead of straight up
items.fix: Keep the outside fingers vertical and slightly inward; the thumb provides outward pressure only to thin, not to flare
